summary: Atwood explores the tension between rigid methodologies and expert intuition in software development, using the chef/recipe metaphor. He pushes back against James Bach's dismissal of maturity models as worthless, arguing that recipes have value even for experts who occasionally need to reference them. The key insight is that both novice-friendly recipes and expert-level flexibility should coexist, avoiding religious adherence to any single methodology. He advocates for providing baseline practices while also offering alternatives for those who've outgrown them.
key_points: Instructions appropriate for novices may be inappropriate for experts, and vice versa
Dismissing all recipes/methodologies as worthless is as wrongheaded as demanding strict adherence to them
Even expert chefs occasionally benefit from referring to recipe cards
The solution is coexistence: start with common denominator recipes but provide alternatives for advanced practitioners
money_quotes: Instructions that are appropriate for a novice may be totally inappropriate for an expert.
A maturity model is basically a gang of best practices hooked on crystal meth.
The idea that a recipe (e.g., a best practice, a methodology, or a maturity model) is completely worthless is just as wrongheaded as the idea that everything should be based on a strict recipe.
Even an expert chef may occasionally find it helpful to refer to a recipe card.
The point is to avoid an onerous, religious one-methodology-fits-all view of the world.
key_insight: Best practices and methodologies have value at every skill level, but should be treated as helpful references rather than religious mandates—the goal is flexible coexistence, not dogmatic adherence.
